params.text:
The "never lift" approach and banned techniques

Modern care follows a "never lift" (minimal lifting) approach: staff should not manually lift the full weight of a person unless there is no safe alternative, such as a genuine emergency. Instead, we use equipment and encourage the person to do what they safely can for themselves.

Some old techniques are now considered dangerous and unacceptable except in true life-or-death emergencies:

  • Drag lift (underarm lift): hooking your arms under a person's armpits to haul them up — it can damage shoulders, joints and fragile skin. The Royal College of Nursing regards it as a form of abuse.
  • Pulling a person up by their arms out of a chair or bed — risks dislocation and skin tears.
  • The "orthodox" or "cradle" lift where a person clings around the carer's neck — risks falls and injury to both people.

Person-centred handling and care plans

Every person is an individual. A good moving-and-handling plan is person-centred: it records how that specific person should be moved, what equipment to use, how much they can do themselves, and their own preferences and dignity.

  • Always read and follow the person's moving-and-handling care plan before you help them.
  • Explain what you are going to do, gain consent, and encourage the person to help where they safely can.
  • Tell a senior member of staff if a person's needs change — the plan may need reassessing.

params.question: A colleague suggests lifting a resident from a chair by hooking your arms under their armpits. What should you do?

answers.text: Refuse and use the method in the care plan, such as a hoist or a safe assisted stand

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. The underarm (drag) lift is a banned technique. Follow the person's care plan and use the safe, trained method.

answers.text: Go ahead, as it is quicker and the resident is light

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No — the drag lift can damage shoulders, joints and skin and is regarded as abusive. Never use it.

answers.text: Do it once now and mention it to the manager later

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No — a banned technique should not be used at all outside a genuine emergency. Use the safe method every time.

answers.text: Ask the resident to wrap their arms around your neck instead

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No — that is also an unsafe technique that risks falls and injury to both of you.
